experiment: what-happened-when readout through a frozen LLM (content-binding language capstone)
finding: Both fields are individually readable from the bound cortex code through the frozen LLM, but a JOINT single answer ('<...> <...>') has a CAPACITY TRADEOFF: the LLM reads whichever field training emphasizes, and what/when trade off.

what.note: marginal -- WHAT got only ~half the training share

interpretation: The cortex BINDS both (CPU decode: what 100%, when 1.31 steps -- the neuroscience result). Through the frozen LLM, EACH field is independently readable to SIGNIFICANCE -- WHAT 76% p=0.033 (event-emphasized); WHEN 67-91% p=0.033 (time-emphasized or fair share) -- but a single small LoRA readout cannot max BOTH at once: emphasizing/starving one trades off the other (joint event-first -> WHAT; joint time-first -> WHEN; separate 50/50 -> WHEN sig, WHAT marginal from the halved training share). This is a capacity/training-share property of the readout INTERFACE, not of the binding.
